text: We present our experience with laparoscopic radical nephrectomy with inferior vena cava thrombectomy.
We present the case of a sixty five year old male patient with no relevant clinical history.
He complained of progressive loss of weight up to ten kilograms, edema in both lower limbs, and denied hematuria or pain.
CT scan of abdomen and pelvis showed a solid infiltrating mass occupying the middle and lower third of the right kidney, which enhanced with contrast.
Invasion of the right renal vein and inferior vena cava below the diaphragm was present.
The patient is positioned in lateral decubitus, and three trocars are inserted along the midline: two 12-millimeter trocars and one 5-millimeter trocar.
An accessory subcostal trocar measuring twelve millimeters is placed in the patient's abdominal region, complementing the existing trochars for the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins by releasing the lower border of the liver, likely using surgical instruments to separate it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the inferior vena cava, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects the right renal artery at the intercaval aortic level, followed by early clamping of the artery.
Four hepatic veins, tributaries of the caudate lobe, are visible on the anterior face of the inferior vena cava.
The surgeon links and sections the identified hepatic veins, which are tributaries of the caudate lobe, at the anterior face of the inferior vena cava.
The surgeon progresses above the upper limit of the thrombus and places a Rommel's tourniquet around the vessel to control blood flow.
The surgeon adjusts the Rommel tourniquet around the identified inferior vena cava, ensuring proper placement for vascular control.
The surgeon removes the vascular clamps from the previously clamped vessels, completing the step of releasing vascular control.
In conclusion, laparoscopic radical nephrectomy with inferior vena cava thrombectomy is a challenging but feasible procedure in selected patients.
